quote.text: In every walk with nature, one receives far more than he seeks.
quote.author: John Muir

fun_fact.text: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards! Their wings can rotate in a full circle, allowing them to hover in place and even fly upside down for short distances. The Pacific Northwest is home to several species, including the beautiful rufous hummingbird that visits Port Angeles every summer.

short_read.title: The Secret Language of Trees
short_read.teaser: Scientists have discovered that trees communicate with each other through underground fungal networks, sharing nutrients and warning each other of dangers. This 'wood wide web' shows that forests are more connected than we ever imagined.

medium_read.title: The Golden Age of Hungarian Thermal Baths
medium_read.teaser: Hungary has over 1,000 natural thermal springs, and bathing culture has been part of daily life for centuries. From Roman times through the Ottoman Empire to today, these healing waters remain a cherished tradition.

long_read.title: The Women Who Mapped the Ocean Floor
long_read.teaser: Marie Tharp created the first scientific map of the Atlantic Ocean floor in the 1950s, discovering the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and revolutionizing our understanding of plate tectonics. Her story is one of persistence, brilliance, and overcoming barriers in a male-dominated field.

port_angeles.title: Harbor Seal Pupping Season
port_angeles.description: July is peak pupping season for harbor seals in the Strait of Juan de Fuca! Mother seals can be spotted nursing their pups on rocky shores and beaches around Port Angeles. If you see a pup alone on the beach, it's likely just resting while mom hunts nearby—observing from a distance is the best way to help.

puzzle_1.question: A hummingbird's heart beats approximately ___ times per minute while flying.
puzzle_1.correct_response: Wonderful! Yes, 1,200 beats per minute—that's about 20 times per second! Their tiny hearts work incredibly hard to power their amazing flying abilities.
puzzle_1.close_response: You're very close! A hummingbird's heart beats about 1,200 times per minute while flying—an absolutely astonishing rate!
puzzle_1.wrong_response: A hummingbird's heart actually beats about 1,200 times per minute while flying! It's hard to imagine something so tiny working so fast.
puzzle_1.learn_more_text: To fuel this incredible metabolism, hummingbirds must eat about half their body weight in sugar each day, visiting hundreds of flowers. At night, they enter a hibernation-like state called torpor to conserve energy.

puzzle_2.question: Which Hungarian word means 'butterfly'?
puzzle_2.correct_response: Beautifully done! Pillangó is Hungarian for butterfly—such a lovely, lilting word for such a delicate creature!
puzzle_2.wrong_response: The Hungarian word for butterfly is 'pillangó'—doesn't it sound almost as graceful as the creature itself? (Virág means flower, madár means bird, and napsugár means sunbeam.)
puzzle_2.learn_more_text: Hungarian has many poetic nature words. The word 'pillangó' may come from 'pillant' (to glance) because butterflies seem to flit and glance from flower to flower.

puzzle_3.question: What type of tree is the largest living organism on Earth by volume?
puzzle_3.correct_response: Absolutely right! The Giant Sequoia named 'General Sherman' is the largest living organism by volume—it weighs about 2.7 million pounds and is still growing!
puzzle_3.wrong_response: It's actually the Giant Sequoia! The largest one, named 'General Sherman' in California, weighs about 2.7 million pounds. While coast redwoods are taller, sequoias are more massive overall.
puzzle_3.learn_more_text: Giant Sequoias can live for over 3,000 years and their bark can be up to 3 feet thick, protecting them from fire and insects. They only grow naturally in a small area of California's Sierra Nevada mountains.

game.title: Bubble Shooter
game.description: A relaxing bubble puzzle game—aim and pop matching colored bubbles. Easy to pick up, fun to play!
